description: Source-of-truth manifest for the Market Indicators tab. Every series has an expected title that the validator confirms against the live provider before scraping. chartId groups series that should render on the same chart panel; chartLabel is the legend label inside that chart. Unit codes map to formatters in web/src/indicator-chart.js: percent | dollar | dollar_millions | index | units | persons | ratio | balance_of_opinion.

mortgage_rates.title: Posted mortgage rates
mortgage_rates.description: Posted residential mortgage rates from chartered banks. Rising rates reduce buyer affordability and tighten investment-property cash flows, putting downward pressure on values; falling rates widen affordability, support price growth, and trigger refinancing activity. The variable line (broker average) often leads posted fixed rates by a few months.

goc_yields.title: Government of Canada bond yields
goc_yields.description: Benchmark Government of Canada bond yields. The 5-year is the canonical cap-rate anchor for multi-family and commercial appraisals — appraisers typically add 200–400 bps to derive an indicative cap rate. Rising yields push cap rates higher and depress property values; falling yields compress cap rates and support valuations.

policy_rates.title: Policy & prime rates
policy_rates.description: Bank of Canada policy rate (the overnight target) alongside the chartered-bank prime rate. The policy rate flows into variable mortgages within weeks and into posted fixed rates within months; prime sits a roughly fixed spread (~2.2 pp) above it and is the base for variable mortgages, HELOCs, and most construction / land financing — making it the direct cost-of-capital reference for development pro formas. Rising rates foreshadow softer transaction volumes; cuts often precede a rebound in activity. (CORRA was removed as a redundant indicator — it tracks the target rate within a few basis points by design.)

arrears.title: Mortgage arrears (90+ days)
arrears.description: Share of residential mortgages 90+ days in arrears. A leading indicator of distressed-sale supply: persistent rises typically precede increased forced sales, which appraisers should consider when bracketing comparable sales. Currently disabled — CBA data source is WAF-blocked; Phase 1.5 will source from CMHC or StatsCan.

slos_mortgage.title: SLOS — Mortgage lending conditions
slos_mortgage.description: Bank of Canada Senior Loan Officer Survey, balance-of-opinion index. Positive readings mean lenders are tightening (harder to qualify, lower LTV, stricter stress test); negative readings mean easing. Tightening foreshadows fewer marginal buyers and softer demand at the bottom of the market.

slos_business.title: SLOS — Business lending conditions
slos_business.description: Same survey but for commercial lending — critical for commercial appraisers. Positive readings indicate banks pulling back on CRE lending: deals stall, cap rates drift wider as buyers face tighter financing. Negative readings signal a healthier transaction environment with more comparables to choose from.

insolvency_construction.title: Construction insolvencies (trailing 12 mo.)
insolvency_construction.description: Business insolvencies (bankruptcies + proposals) filed by firms in the Construction sector (NAICS 23), summed over the trailing 12 months, from the Office of the Superintendent of Bankruptcy. Builder/developer failures are a leading source of distressed land and partially-built project sales — a rising trend foreshadows forced dispositions and softer land values that appraisers should weigh when bracketing comparables. The 12-month rolling sum smooths the noisy monthly counts (Manitoba's are small). The Colliers land report flags construction-sector distress as a 2025 theme; this is the publicly-sourced, auto-updating analogue to its proprietary distress-sale counts.

insolvency_real_estate.title: Real-estate insolvencies (trailing 12 mo.)
insolvency_real_estate.description: Business insolvencies (bankruptcies + proposals) filed by firms in the Real Estate and Rental and Leasing sector (NAICS 53), trailing-12-month sum, from the Office of the Superintendent of Bankruptcy. Captures distress among landlords, property managers, and real-estate operating companies — a direct read on owner-side financial stress that precedes receivership sales of income property. Rising counts widen the pool of motivated/forced sellers; use as a credit-stress overlay alongside the SLOS business-lending and arrears indicators.

nhpi.title: New Housing Price Index
nhpi.description: Statistics Canada's New Housing Price Index — builder list prices for new SFD homes, excluding land. Rising NHPI indicates pricing power on new construction; broader resale market typically follows with a 3–6 month lag. Useful for time-adjusting comparable sales and as a feasibility check against rising construction costs.

nhpi_yoy.title: NHPI — year-over-year % change
nhpi_yoy.description: Annualised year-over-year change in NHPI. The 12-month growth rate is the standard time-adjustment factor appraisers apply to dated comparable sales: a sale 12 months ago + the YoY % moves the comparable to today's price level (subject to all the usual caveats about local versus index applicability).

cpi_shelter.title: CPI — Shelter component
cpi_shelter.description: Shelter component of the Consumer Price Index — captures rented + owned shelter inflation, including operating costs (utilities, insurance, property taxes). Rising values mean operating costs are climbing for landlords; flat nominal rents against rising shelter-CPI imply declining real rents. Useful for stabilising operating-expense assumptions in multi-family pro formas.

permits_residential.title: Residential building permits ($)
permits_residential.description: Seasonally-adjusted dollar value of residential building permits issued. A leading indicator of supply: surges in permits typically translate to completions 18–36 months out, putting downward pressure on rents and prices in that timeframe. Sustained low permit volumes signal scarcity ahead and support property values.

permits_nonresidential.title: Non-residential building permits ($)
permits_nonresidential.description: Seasonally-adjusted dollar value of industrial, commercial, and institutional permits. Tracks the commercial development pipeline — strong permits indicate investor confidence and incoming supply for office / retail / industrial space. Weak permits signal caution; useful context when stress-testing absorption assumptions in commercial appraisals.

population.title: Population estimates
population.description: Quarterly population estimates, Canada and all provinces. The fundamental demand driver for residential markets — strong population growth supports rents, occupancy, and prices; flat or declining population signals demand weakness. Net interprovincial migration is the most volatile component for Manitoba. (Shows every province regardless of the geography toggle.)

immigration.title: Immigration (landed immigrants)
immigration.description: Quarterly landed-immigrant counts by province (StatsCan components of international migration). Immigration is a dominant driver of household formation and rental demand — sustained high inflows tighten vacancy and support rent growth; a slowdown (e.g. from federal target cuts) eases demand pressure. Compare a province against Canada to gauge relative demand momentum. Excludes non-permanent residents (students/temporary workers), shown separately in the next chart.

non_permanent_residents.title: Non-permanent residents (net change)
non_permanent_residents.description: Quarterly NET change in non-permanent residents by province (StatsCan components of international migration) — international students, temporary foreign workers, other permit holders, and asylum claimants. Since 2022 this has been the single largest driver of Canada's population growth, far outrunning permanent immigration by volume, and the main explanation for the vacancy compression and rent acceleration of that period. It is highly policy-sensitive: the 2024 federal study-permit cap and temporary-resident targets are pulling the flow down sharply — it can go negative as departures exceed arrivals — which eases rental demand at the margin. Read alongside landed immigration: NPR is the volatile component that permanent-resident counts miss, and the Colliers land report flags its slowdown as a key demand headwind.

unemployment_rate.title: Unemployment rate (15+, SA)
unemployment_rate.description: Seasonally-adjusted unemployment rate for the working-age population. A rising rate signals labour-market weakness that typically softens rental demand (renters lose jobs first) and slows the homebuyer pool. Falling rates indicate tight labour markets, supporting rent growth and price appreciation, though extreme tightness can flag overheating.

employment.title: Total employment (15+, SA)
employment.description: Total persons employed, seasonally adjusted. Useful as a job-creation count: positive year-over-year change indicates net hiring, which feeds household formation and rental absorption. For commercial appraisers, the trajectory in office-using industries is the key sub-signal — flagged for Phase 2b.

weekly_earnings.title: Average weekly earnings (SEPH)
weekly_earnings.description: Survey of Employment, Payrolls and Hours — average weekly earnings, all employees including overtime, industrial aggregate. The dollar-income side of affordability. Rising earnings expand the buyer pool and rent-paying capacity; flat or shrinking earnings against rising rent / mortgage payments signal affordability stress.

weekly_earnings_yoy.title: Wage growth — YoY %
weekly_earnings_yoy.description: Year-over-year % change in average weekly earnings. Compare against rent growth and CPI Shelter to gauge whether wages are keeping pace with shelter costs. Positive wage growth above shelter-CPI inflation = real-income gains for renters and homeowners; the inverse signals affordability deterioration even if both nominal values are rising.

rent_vs_wage_yoy.title: Rent vs wage growth — YoY %
rent_vs_wage_yoy.description: Side-by-side year-over-year % change in average rent (CMHC RMS, Manitoba + Winnipeg) and average weekly earnings (StatsCan SEPH, Manitoba + Canada). When wage lines sit above rent lines, household affordability is improving on average; when rent outpaces wages, rent burdens are rising and renter demand can soften. Useful as a sustainability check for multi-family rent forecasts. Note: CMHC RMS is annual (October snapshot) so rent points are sparse; wages are monthly.

bcpi_residential.title: BCPI — Residential construction cost
bcpi_residential.description: Building Construction Price Index — residential composite, indexed to 2023=100. Captures contractor pricing for the full divisional bundle (concrete, framing, HVAC, finishes, etc.) on residential new construction. Rising BCPI directly raises the replacement-cost approach output and pressures developer margins; falling BCPI improves feasibility on new builds.

bcpi_residential_yoy.title: BCPI Residential — year-over-year % change
bcpi_residential_yoy.description: Annualised YoY change in residential BCPI. The going-rate of construction-cost inflation — directly feeds your replacement-cost approach update frequency, and tells you whether contractor pricing is accelerating or cooling. Useful when reviewing the inflation assumption baked into prior-year insurance-replacement updates.

bcpi_nonresidential.title: BCPI — Non-residential construction cost
bcpi_nonresidential.description: Building Construction Price Index — non-residential composite for office, retail, industrial, and institutional builds. The primary input to the replacement-cost approach for commercial appraisals. Track quarter-over-quarter movement when stress-testing depreciated replacement cost values; trended changes also feed insurance-replacement updates.

bcpi_nonresidential_yoy.title: BCPI Non-residential — year-over-year % change
bcpi_nonresidential_yoy.description: Annualised YoY change in non-residential BCPI. Same indicator as the residential YoY but for the office/retail/industrial/institutional cost bundle — the most direct signal of whether commercial replacement-cost trended numbers are still defensible at last year's depreciation assumption.

crspi.title: Commercial rents (CRSPI)
crspi.description: Statistics Canada Commercial Rents Services Price Index — change over time in net effective lease rates ($/sq ft) for occupied office, retail, and industrial space, indexed to 2019=100. The income-approach counterpart to the residential rent series: rising CRSPI signals strengthening commercial lease economics that support higher capitalized values; flat or falling readings warn of softening rents. Winnipeg + Manitoba + national lines let you place the local market against the Canadian trend. Total-index history runs from 2006; the building-type splits start 2019.

crspi_building_type.title: Commercial rents by building type — Canada
crspi_building_type.description: CRSPI decomposed by building type at the national level (office, retail, industrial & warehouse), 2019=100. StatsCan publishes the building-type split for Canada only — not for Winnipeg — but the divergence between sectors (e.g. industrial outrunning office through the 2020s) is a useful read on which asset classes carry rent momentum when bracketing commercial comparables or selecting a market-rent growth rate by property type.

crspi_yoy.title: Commercial rents — year-over-year % change
crspi_yoy.description: Annualised year-over-year change in the total CRSPI for Winnipeg and Canada. The growth rate is the commercial-rent analogue of the wage / residential-rent YoY indicators — use it to time-adjust dated lease comparables, or to sanity-check the rent-growth assumption baked into a multi-year commercial income pro forma.

cap_rate_pressure.title: Cap-rate pressure (5-yr GoC + spread)
cap_rate_pressure.description: The 5-year Government of Canada bond yield with 200 / 300 / 400 bps overlays — a rough scaffolding for the cap-rate range an institutional buyer would underwrite. Pick the spread that fits your asset class (multi-family closer to 200-250, well-located commercial 300, secondary commercial 350+). Use these as a first sanity check on cap-rate inputs in income-approach work; replace with deal-specific spreads when comparables exist.

feasibility.title: Construction feasibility — NHPI ÷ BCPI
feasibility.description: Ratio of new-home prices (NHPI) to residential construction cost (BCPI), with both indexed so the ratio is interpretable as relative momentum. Rising means new-home prices are outpacing construction costs — feasibility improving for developers. Falling means costs are squeezing margins; expect builders to slow starts or push for price increases. Caveat: this is a national/CMA composite — local land cost and the lot premium are not captured.

mortgage_payment.title: Monthly P&I — representative residential mortgage
mortgage_payment.description: Monthly principal & interest on a representative $400,000 residential mortgage, 25-year amortization, at the prevailing 5-year posted rate from Bank of Canada. Useful as a 'where is the household affordability ceiling moving' read — for the same loan principal, this shows the dollar impact of every rate move. Rising payment = compressed buyer pool; falling = restored affordability.

construction_per_capita.title: Construction $ per capita (permits ÷ population)
construction_per_capita.description: Monthly residential building-permit dollar value divided by quarterly provincial population, indexed per resident. A relative-investment indicator: rising means construction activity is outpacing population growth (more building per person), which usually precedes increased supply. Falling means activity is lagging demographics; if sustained, expect rent pressure.

retail_trade.title: Retail trade sales
retail_trade.description: Seasonally-adjusted monthly retail trade sales in Manitoba (StatsCan table 20-10-0056). A broad read on household spending and consumer confidence; month-over-month moves are the headline the Province cites. Rising retail sales signal a healthy consumer economy that supports population retention and rental demand.

manufacturing_sales.title: Manufacturing sales
manufacturing_sales.description: Seasonally-adjusted monthly sales of goods manufactured (shipments) in Manitoba (StatsCan table 16-10-0048). A trade-exposed sector sensitive to tariffs and external demand; sustained declines flag economic headwinds for the broader provincial economy.

wholesale_trade.title: Wholesale trade sales
wholesale_trade.description: Seasonally-adjusted monthly wholesale trade sales in Manitoba (StatsCan table 20-10-0074). An upstream indicator of goods demand; tracks alongside retail and manufacturing as part of the provincial activity picture.

farm_cash.title: Farm cash receipts
farm_cash.description: Annual Manitoba farm cash receipts (StatsCan table 32-10-0045), split into total / crop / livestock. Agriculture is a pillar of the provincial economy; crop and livestock receipts swing with commodity prices and trade access (e.g. canola tariffs).

cpi_allitems.title: CPI — All-items
cpi_allitems.description: Manitoba All-items Consumer Price Index (StatsCan table 18-10-0004), the headline inflation gauge. The year-over-year change is the inflation rate the Province quotes; cooling inflation eases cost-of-living pressure on households.

housing_starts.title: Housing starts (all areas)
housing_starts.description: Monthly CMHC housing starts for Manitoba, all areas (StatsCan table 34-10-0143). Year-to-date totals versus the prior year are the standard read on the residential construction pipeline; sustained gains add future supply.

exports.title: Merchandise exports (domestic)
exports.description: Manitoba domestic merchandise exports to all countries, all commodities (StatsCan table 12-10-0175). A trade-exposed barometer central to the provincial economy — energy, agricultural products, and machinery are major export categories. Year-to-date versus the prior year is the headline read; weakness flags trade-war or external-demand pressure.
